1:
"I exhort therefore, that, first of all, supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks, be made for all men;" The witnesses note the call to pray for all men. Chrysostom notes the priest is the common father, as it were, of all the world, and so should care for all, even as God whom he serves; from praying for all, two advantages result, the first being that hatred toward those without is done away, for none can hate those for whom he prays. The witnesses note Paul exhorting that, first of all, supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings be made for all men; the breadth of public prayer reaching to all mankind — that Paul, as a chief duty of the church's worship, urges the offering of prayer of every kind — "supplications" (humble requests in need), "prayers" (general devotions), "intercessions" (pleadings on others' behalf), and "giving of thanks" (gratitude) — and that "for all men," the whole human family, of every sort and condition, so that the church's heart, like God's, is to be enlarged in compassionate prayer for all; supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks, be made for all men. The witnesses note supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks. The witnesses note be made for all men.

The witnesses note the call to pray for all men — supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings for the whole human family, the church's heart enlarged like God's.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to pray for all. The lesson the expositors draw is that the church is to offer prayer of every kind for all mankind, its heart enlarged in compassion. The witnesses note the exhortation: "I exhort therefore, that, first of all" — Paul urges this as a chief duty; the exhortation; the first of all; the chief duty; the worship; the urged. The expositors observe the kinds of prayer: "supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks" — every kind of prayer; the kinds of prayer; the supplications; the prayers; the intercessions; the thanksgivings. The witnesses press the for all men: "be made for all men" — for all men (the whole human family); the for all men; the the whole family; the every sort; the all conditions; the mankind. The expositors draw the lesson on the breadth: the church's prayer is to reach to all mankind; the breadth; the all mankind; the the church; the reaches; the enlarged. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the priest, like God, should care for all; from praying for all, hatred is done away; the the priest; the care for all; the hatred done away; the prays; the love. The expositors note the heart enlarged: the church's heart, like God's, is enlarged in compassion; the heart enlarged; the like God's; the compassion; the all; the prayer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the supplications prayers intercessions and giving of thanks, the be made for all men, and the prayer for all. The verse thus records the call to pray for all — teaching that the church is to offer prayer of every kind for all mankind, its heart enlarged in compassion.
2:
"For kings, and for all that are in authority; that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty." The witnesses note the duty to pray especially for rulers, that the church may live in peace. Chrysostom notes that if to put an end to public wars the priest is exhorted to pray for kings and governors, much more should private individuals; for there are three grievous kinds of war — public, civil, and the war within the man himself. The witnesses note Paul charging that prayer be made for kings and all in authority, that the church may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty. The witnesses note the prayer for rulers and its aim of a peaceful, godly life — that Paul singles out from "all men" the special object of prayer for "kings and all that are in authority" (even when those rulers were heathen persecutors), and names the end such prayer serves: "that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life" — free from disturbance and persecution — "in all godliness and honesty," that under the shelter of public peace the church might serve God in piety and live in honest seriousness; that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty. The witnesses note for kings, and for all that are in authority. The witnesses note that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty.

The witnesses note the duty to pray for kings and rulers — even heathen ones — that the church may lead a quiet, peaceable life in all godliness and honesty.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer for rulers and its peaceful aim. The lesson the expositors draw is that the church prays for those in authority, that under public peace it may live godly and honest lives. The witnesses note the kings: "for kings, and for all that are in authority" — prayer for kings and all in authority; the kings; the in authority; the rulers; the even heathen; the prayed. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes prayer for rulers ends public wars; the for rulers; the ends wars; the the priest; the much more; the prayed. The witnesses press the quiet life: "that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life" — that the church may lead a quiet, peaceable life; the quiet life; the peaceable; the free from disturbance; the persecution; the the church. The expositors draw the lesson on the godliness: "in all godliness and honesty" — in all godliness (piety) and honesty (honest seriousness); the godliness; the piety; the honesty; the seriousness; the serve God. The witnesses observe the shelter of peace: under the shelter of public peace the church serves God; the shelter of peace; the public peace; the serves God; the the church; the godly. The expositors note the even persecutors: even when the rulers were heathen persecutors, prayer was to be made; the even persecutors; the heathen; the rulers; the prayed; the for them. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for kings and for all that are in authority, the that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and honesty, and the prayer for rulers. The verse thus records the prayer for rulers and its peaceful aim — teaching that the church prays for those in authority, that under public peace it may live godly lives.
3:
"For this is good and acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour;" The witnesses note that such prayer is good and pleasing to God. Gill notes it is good in itself, and good in the sight of an omniscient God, to pray for all sorts of men, even those set in highest places of government, though enemies and persecutors. The witnesses note Paul declaring that this (prayer for all men and for rulers) is good and acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour; the divine approval of wide, compassionate prayer — that Paul confirms the duty by its pleasing God: such large-hearted intercession, even for enemies and persecutors, is "good" (right and excellent in itself) and "acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour" (well-pleasing to him who sees the heart), and the very title "God our Saviour" hints why — for the God who saves and desires the salvation of men delights in the prayers of his people for all; this is good and acceptable in the sight of God our Saviour. The witnesses note this is good and acceptable. The witnesses note in the sight of God our Saviour.

The witnesses note that wide, compassionate prayer is good and acceptable to God our Saviour — well-pleasing to him who saves and desires the salvation of men.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine approval of wide prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is that large-hearted intercession, even for enemies, is good and pleasing to God our Saviour. The witnesses note the good: "this is good" — Gill notes it is good in itself; the good; the right; the excellent; the in itself; the prayer. The expositors observe the acceptable: "and acceptable" — and acceptable (well-pleasing); the acceptable; the well-pleasing; the pleasing; the to God; the approved. The witnesses press the in God's sight: "in the sight of God our Saviour" — in the sight of God our Saviour; the in God's sight; the our Saviour; the who sees; the the heart; the God. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine approval: God approves such wide intercession, even for enemies; the divine approval; the wide intercession; the enemies; the persecutors; the approves. The witnesses observe the God who saves: the title "God our Saviour" hints why — he desires the salvation of men; the God who saves; the our Saviour; the desires salvation; the of men; the delights. The expositors note the delights in prayer: the God who saves delights in his people's prayers for all; the delights; the the prayers; the for all; the his people; the pleased. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this is good and acceptable, the in the sight of God our Saviour, and the divine approval. The verse thus records the divine approval of wide prayer — teaching that large-hearted intercession is good and pleasing to God our Saviour.
4:
"Who will have all men to be saved, and to come unto the knowledge of the truth." The witnesses note God's will concerning the salvation of all men. Gill notes the salvation God wills that all men should enjoy is not a mere possibility or offer, but a real, certain, actual salvation determined for them; and "all men" he takes to mean all sorts and conditions of men, the elect of every kind, not every individual without exception. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God will have all men to be saved and to come to the knowledge of the truth; the much-debated will of God for the salvation of all — that Paul grounds the duty of praying for all men in God's own gracious will toward all; the witnesses note the great debate on this verse — the Reformed reading "all men" as all kinds and conditions of men (Jews and Gentiles, high and low, the elect out of every sort), so that God effectually wills and accomplishes the salvation of all such; while others (the Arminian and general reading) take it of every individual without exception, God sincerely desiring and willing the salvation of all, though all are not saved; both agree that the verse grounds wide prayer in God's saving goodness, which reaches to men of every sort; who will have all men to be saved. The witnesses note who will have all men to be saved. The witnesses note and to come unto the knowledge of the truth.

The witnesses note God's will that all men be saved — debated as all kinds/conditions of men (Reformed) or every individual (Arminian) — grounding wide prayer in God's saving goodness.

So the witnesses make the verse God's will for the salvation of all. The lesson the expositors draw is that wide prayer rests on God's saving will, however "all men" be understood. The witnesses note the will: "who will have all men to be saved" — God wills all men to be saved; the will; the all men; the saved; the God's will; the gracious. The expositors present the debate: the witnesses note the great debate — the Reformed read "all men" as all kinds and conditions (the elect of every sort), God effectually willing their salvation; others (Arminian) take it of every individual, God sincerely desiring all to be saved though not all are; both ground wide prayer in God's saving goodness. The witnesses press the knowledge of truth: "and to come unto the knowledge of the truth" — and to come to the knowledge of the truth; the knowledge of truth; the come to; the the truth; the saved; the brought. The expositors draw the lesson on the Reformed reading: Gill reads "all men" as all sorts, the elect of every kind, whose salvation God determines; the Reformed reading; the all sorts; the the elect; the every kind; the determined. The witnesses observe the other reading: others take it of every individual, God's sincere desire for all; the other reading; the every individual; the sincere desire; the all; the willed. The expositors note the common ground: both agree the verse grounds wide prayer in God's saving goodness to men of every sort; the common ground; the wide prayer; the saving goodness; the every sort; the grounds. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who will have all men to be saved, the and to come unto the knowledge of the truth, and the will for salvation. The verse thus records God's will for the salvation of all — teaching that wide prayer rests on God's saving will, reaching to men of every sort.
5:
"For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus;" The witnesses note the one God and the one Mediator. Gill notes this regards not so much the unity of God in his essence as with respect to men — that there is but one God, the Creator and providential Saviour of all men, of Jews and Gentiles alike, which is a reason why all sorts should be prayed for. The witnesses note Paul declaring that there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus; the one God and one Mediator the ground of universal prayer and salvation — that Paul grounds the breadth of prayer and the breadth of God's saving will in two great unities: there is "one God" (the one Creator and Lord of all men, of every nation) and "one mediator between God and men" — and that Mediator is "the man Christ Jesus," truly man (and, as elsewhere, truly God), the one appointed go-between who alone bridges the gulf between the holy God and sinful men — so that, since there is but one God and one Mediator for all, the prayer and the gospel are to reach to all; one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. The witnesses note for there is one God. The witnesses note and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the one God and one Mediator, the man Christ Jesus — the one go-between who alone bridges the gulf between God and men; the ground of universal prayer.

So the witnesses make the verse the one God and one Mediator. The lesson the expositors draw is that the one God and one Mediator for all ground the breadth of prayer and salvation. The witnesses note the one God: "there is one God" — Gill notes one God, Creator and Saviour of all men; the one God; the Creator; the of all men; the Jews and Gentiles; the one. The expositors observe the ground of prayer: the one God of all is a reason all sorts should be prayed for; the ground of prayer; the one God; the all sorts; the prayed; the reason. The witnesses press the one Mediator: "and one mediator between God and men" — one Mediator between God and men; the one Mediator; the between God and men; the go-between; the bridges; the one. The expositors draw the lesson on the man Christ Jesus: "the man Christ Jesus" — the Mediator is the man Christ Jesus, truly man (and truly God); the the man Christ Jesus; the truly man; the truly God; the the Mediator; the appointed. The witnesses observe the bridges the gulf: the Mediator alone bridges the gulf between the holy God and sinful men; the bridges the gulf; the the holy God; the sinful men; the alone; the go-between. The expositors note the reach to all: since there is one God and one Mediator for all, prayer and gospel reach to all; the reach to all; the one God; the one Mediator; the for all; the universal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for there is one God, the and one mediator between God and men the man Christ Jesus, and the one God and Mediator. The verse thus records the one God and one Mediator — teaching that the one God and one Mediator for all ground the breadth of prayer and salvation.
6:
"Who gave himself a ransom for all, to be testified in due time." The witnesses note Christ giving himself a ransom for all. Gill notes that what the Mediator gave as a ransom was "himself" — his body and soul, both made an offering for sin, his whole human nature in union with his divine person — given voluntarily into the hands of justice and death, which shows his great love. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ gave himself a ransom for all, to be testified in due time; the ransom of Christ given for all, the substance of the gospel testimony — that Paul names the work of the one Mediator: he "gave himself" (his whole self, body and soul, in his sacrifice) as "a ransom" (a price of redemption, paid to deliver captives) "for all"; the witnesses note that this "for all" is read, with v.4, either as all kinds of men (the ransom certainly redeeming the elect of every sort) or as a ransom of universal sufficiency offered to all; and Paul adds that this great fact was "to be testified in due time" — published as the gospel's central witness in God's appointed season — so that the redeeming death of Christ is the truth to be proclaimed to all; who gave himself a ransom for all. The witnesses note who gave himself a ransom for all. The witnesses note to be testified in due time.

The witnesses note Christ giving himself a ransom for all — his whole self the price of redemption — to be testified as the gospel's central witness in due time.

So the witnesses make the verse the ransom of Christ for all. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ gave himself as a ransom, the central truth of the gospel to be proclaimed to all. The witnesses note the gave himself: "who gave himself" — Gill notes Christ gave himself, body and soul, in sacrifice; the gave himself; the body and soul; the his whole self; the the sacrifice; the offered. The expositors observe the ransom: "a ransom" — a ransom (a price of redemption to deliver captives); the ransom; the price; the redemption; the captives; the deliver. The witnesses press the for all: "for all" — for all; the witnesses note this is read as all kinds of men (the elect of every sort) or as a ransom of universal sufficiency; the for all; the all kinds; the the elect; the universal sufficiency; the debated. The expositors draw the lesson on the redemption: the ransom delivers captives from sin and death; the redemption; the delivers; the captives; the sin and death; the the price. The witnesses observe the testified: "to be testified in due time" — to be testified (published) in due time; the testified; the published; the due time; the appointed season; the witness. The expositors note the gospel's witness: the redeeming death is the gospel's central witness to all; the gospel's witness; the the redeeming death; the central; the proclaimed; the all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who gave himself a ransom for all, the to be testified in due time, and the ransom of Christ. The verse thus records the ransom of Christ for all — teaching that Christ gave himself as a ransom, the central truth of the gospel to be proclaimed to all.
7:
"Whereunto I am ordained a preacher, and an apostle, (I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not;) a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity." The witnesses note Paul's ordination as preacher, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles. Gill notes Paul was ordained or appointed to be a preacher of the gospel from all eternity, separated to it in time, and put into the ministry by Christ himself — not a common or ordinary preacher, but an apostle, an extraordinary officer in the church. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he was ordained a preacher and apostle, and a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity, affirming with an oath that he speaks the truth. The witnesses note Paul's commission to proclaim the ransom to the Gentiles — that Paul, having named the universal ransom "to be testified," points to his own office as the very channel of that testimony: he was "ordained a preacher" (a herald of the good news), "an apostle" (a sent one of Christ), and specifically "a teacher of the Gentiles" — the great instance of God's saving will reaching beyond the Jews — and he teaches them "in faith and verity" (in the true faith and truth); and so weighty is his claim that he seals it with a solemn asseveration, "I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not"; a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity. The witnesses note I am ordained a preacher, and an apostle. The witnesses note a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity.

The witnesses note Paul ordained preacher, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles — the channel of the universal testimony, the saving will reaching beyond the Jews.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's commission to the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul was ordained to proclaim the universal ransom, especially to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the ordained: "I am ordained a preacher, and an apostle" — Gill notes Paul ordained a preacher and apostle by Christ; the ordained; the preacher; the apostle; the by Christ; the appointed. The expositors observe the herald and sent one: a preacher (herald) and apostle (sent one of Christ); the herald; the sent one; the preacher; the apostle; the office. The witnesses press the teacher of the Gentiles: "a teacher of the Gentiles" — specifically a teacher of the Gentiles; the teacher of the Gentiles; the the Gentiles; the beyond the Jews; the saving will; the channel. The expositors draw the lesson on the channel: Paul is the channel of the universal testimony; the channel; the testimony; the the ransom; the Gentiles; the proclaim. The witnesses observe the in faith and verity: "in faith and verity" — teaching them in the true faith and truth; the in faith and verity; the true faith; the truth; the teaches; the sound. The expositors note the oath: "I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not" — Paul seals his claim with a solemn oath; the oath; the speak the truth; the lie not; the solemn; the asseveration. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I am ordained a preacher and an apostle, the a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and verity, and the commission to the Gentiles. The verse thus records Paul's commission to the Gentiles — teaching that Paul was ordained to proclaim the universal ransom, especially to the Gentiles.
8:
"I will therefore that men pray every where, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting." The witnesses note the call for men to pray everywhere with holy hands. Chrysostom notes Christ's word that the hypocrites love to pray to be seen of men, but the believer is to enter his closet; yet here Paul speaks of public prayer, requiring not the place only but the inward purity, "holy hands, without wrath and doubting." The witnesses note Paul willing that men pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting; the manner of acceptable prayer — purity, peace, and faith — that Paul, returning to the duty of prayer, directs how and where it is to be offered: "every where" (no longer confined, as under the law, to the temple, but in all places), by "men" (the public leading of prayer in the assembly belonging to them), and that with the right disposition — "lifting up holy hands" (the hands a symbol of a pure life, the worshipper clean from defiling sin), "without wrath" (free from anger and malice toward others), and "without doubting" (in the confidence of faith, not wavering) — so that acceptable prayer requires a holy life, a loving heart, and a believing trust; lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting. The witnesses note I will therefore that men pray every where. The witnesses note lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting.

The witnesses note the call to pray everywhere with holy hands, without wrath and doubting — acceptable prayer requiring a holy life, a loving heart, and believing trust.

So the witnesses make the verse the manner of acceptable prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is that prayer is to be offered everywhere with purity, peace, and faith. The witnesses note the everywhere: "I will therefore that men pray every where" — no longer confined to the temple, but in all places; the everywhere; the all places; the not the temple; the men; the public. The expositors observe the men: the public leading of prayer belonging to men; the men; the public; the leading; the the assembly; the prayer. The witnesses press the holy hands: "lifting up holy hands" — the hands a symbol of a pure life; the holy hands; the symbol; the pure life; the clean; the worshipper. The expositors draw the lesson on the without wrath: "without wrath" — free from anger and malice; the without wrath; the no anger; the no malice; the loving; the peace. The witnesses observe the without doubting: "and doubting" — in the confidence of faith, not wavering; the without doubting; the confidence; the faith; the not wavering; the believing. The expositors note the threefold disposition: acceptable prayer requires a holy life, a loving heart, and believing trust; the threefold disposition; the holy life; the loving heart; the believing trust; the acceptable. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will therefore that men pray every where, the lifting up holy hands without wrath and doubting, and the manner of prayer. The verse thus records the manner of acceptable prayer — teaching that prayer is to be offered everywhere with purity, peace, and faith.
9:
"In like manner also, that women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and sobriety; not with broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array;" The witnesses note the call for women to adorn themselves modestly. Gill notes that as the men were to pray, so the women likewise are to join in public prayer with purity of heart and hand, without murmuring toward God and without wrath toward others, and in faith; and the apostle proceeds to direct their adorning. The witnesses note Paul directing that women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and sobriety, not with elaborate hair, gold, pearls, or costly array; the modesty and sobriety that should mark Christian women's dress — that Paul, turning from the men's prayer to the women, directs the manner of their adorning: they are to be clothed "in modest apparel" (decent, becoming dress), and inwardly with "shamefacedness" (a chaste modesty and reverence) and "sobriety" (sound self-restraint), and not to glory in outward show — "broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array" — the extravagant ornament that betrays vanity, so that the Christian woman's true ornament is inward and moral, not a gaudy display; women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and sobriety. The witnesses note women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and sobriety. The witnesses note not with broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array.

The witnesses note the call for women to adorn themselves modestly, with shamefacedness and sobriety, not with costly show — the true ornament inward and moral, not gaudy.

So the witnesses make the verse the modesty that should mark women's dress. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Christian woman's adorning is modest and sober, her true ornament inward, not extravagant display. The witnesses note the in like manner: "in like manner also" — Gill notes as the men prayed, the women likewise; the in like manner; the the women; the likewise; the join; the prayer. The expositors observe the modest apparel: "that women adorn themselves in modest apparel" — in modest (decent, becoming) apparel; the modest apparel; the decent; the becoming; the dress; the clothed. The witnesses press the shamefacedness: "with shamefacedness and sobriety" — with shamefacedness (chaste modesty) and sobriety (self-restraint); the shamefacedness; the chaste modesty; the reverence; the sobriety; the restraint. The expositors draw the lesson on the not costly: "not with broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array" — not with elaborate hair, gold, pearls, or costly array; the not costly; the broided hair; the gold; the pearls; the array. The witnesses observe the vanity: extravagant ornament betrays vanity; the vanity; the extravagant; the ornament; the show; the gaudy. The expositors note the inward ornament: the true ornament is inward and moral, not display; the inward ornament; the moral; the not display; the true; the inward. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the women adorn themselves in modest apparel with shamefacedness and sobriety, the not with broided hair or gold or pearls or costly array, and the modesty of dress. The verse thus records the modesty that should mark women's dress — teaching that the Christian woman's adorning is modest and sober, her ornament inward, not extravagant.
10:
"But (which becometh women professing godliness) with good works." The witnesses note the true adorning of godly women — good works. Gill notes that what becomes women professing godliness is not any particular grace only, nor outward holiness of life alone, but the whole of religion, internal and external — and that their true ornament is good works. The witnesses note Paul declaring that women professing godliness should adorn themselves with good works; the inward and moral ornament fitting for godly women — that Paul, having forbidden the gaudy outward ornament, names the true adorning that "becometh women professing godliness" (those who make profession of the fear and worship of God): not jewels and costly array, but "good works" — deeds of piety, charity, and holiness — which are the fitting and beautiful ornament of a Christian woman, an adorning that answers to her profession and pleases God; with good works, which becometh women professing godliness. The witnesses note which becometh women professing godliness. The witnesses note with good works.

The witnesses note the true adorning of godly women — good works, not jewels; the fitting ornament that answers to a woman's profession of godliness and pleases God.

So the witnesses make the verse the ornament of good works. The lesson the expositors draw is that the godly woman's true adorning is good works, fitting her profession, not outward display. The witnesses note the professing godliness: "which becometh women professing godliness" — Gill notes those who profess the fear and worship of God; the professing godliness; the the profession; the fear of God; the worship; the godly. The expositors observe the the whole of religion: not one grace only, but the whole of religion, internal and external; the the whole of religion; the not one grace; the internal; the external; the all. The witnesses press the good works: "with good works" — adorned with good works; the good works; the deeds; the piety; the charity; the holiness. The expositors draw the lesson on the fitting ornament: good works are the fitting and beautiful ornament; the fitting ornament; the beautiful; the the adorning; the good works; the becoming. The witnesses observe the answers to profession: the adorning answers to her profession; the answers to profession; the her profession; the godliness; the fitting; the becomes. The expositors note the pleases God: this adorning pleases God; the pleases God; the well-pleasing; the the true; the ornament; the godly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which becometh women professing godliness, the with good works, and the ornament of good works. The verse thus records the ornament of good works — teaching that the godly woman's true adorning is good works, fitting her profession, not outward display.
11:
"Let the woman learn in silence with all subjection." The witnesses note the charge that women learn quietly and in submission. Chrysostom notes that great modesty and propriety the blessed Paul requires of women, not only in dress but even in speech, saying "let the woman learn in silence," as he also wrote to the Corinthians, "it is a shame for women to speak in the church." The witnesses note Paul charging that the woman learn in silence with all subjection; the quiet, submissive learning enjoined upon women in the assembly — that Paul, regulating order in the public worship, directs that "the woman" is to "learn" (a positive thing — she is to be taught and to grow) but "in silence" (quietly, not taking the public teaching role in the assembly) and "with all subjection" (in due submission to the order God has set and to those who teach) — so that her place in the gathered church is that of a quiet, teachable learner, not a public instructor; let the woman learn in silence with all subjection. The witnesses note let the woman learn in silence. The witnesses note with all subjection.

The witnesses note the charge that the woman learn in silence with all subjection — taught and growing, yet quietly, not taking the public teaching role in the assembly.

So the witnesses make the verse the quiet, submissive learning of women. The lesson the expositors draw is that the woman is to learn quietly and in submission, not taking the public teaching role in the assembly. The witnesses note the learn: "let the woman learn" — the woman is to learn (a positive thing, taught and growing); the learn; the taught; the growing; the positive; the instructed. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul requires modesty even in speech, as to the Corinthians; the modesty; the in speech; the the Corinthians; the silence; the propriety. The witnesses press the in silence: "in silence" — quietly, not taking the public teaching role; the in silence; the quietly; the not public teaching; the the assembly; the quiet. The expositors draw the lesson on the subjection: "with all subjection" — with all subjection (due submission to order); the subjection; the submission; the the order; the God's; the due. The witnesses observe the teachable learner: her place is that of a quiet, teachable learner; the teachable learner; the quiet; the her place; the the church; the learner. The expositors note the not public instructor: not a public instructor in the assembly; the not public instructor; the not the teacher; the the assembly; the silence; the order. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let the woman learn in silence, the with all subjection, and the quiet learning. The verse thus records the quiet, submissive learning of women — teaching that the woman is to learn quietly and in submission, not taking the public teaching role.
12:
"But I suffer not a woman to teach, nor to usurp authority over the man, but to be in silence." The witnesses note the limit set on women teaching or ruling over men in the church. Gill notes that women may teach in private, in their own houses and families — they are to be teachers of good things, to bring up their children in the nurture of the Lord, and a mother's instruction is not to be forsaken — but Paul forbids the public teaching of and authority over men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he suffers not a woman to teach nor to usurp authority over the man, but to be in silence; the restriction of public teaching and authority, with its sphere defined — that Paul, defining the woman's role in the assembly, "suffers not" (does not permit) a woman publicly "to teach" (the men in the church) "nor to usurp authority over the man" (to exercise rule and dominion over him), but bids her "be in silence" in that public sphere; the witnesses note that this is not a forbidding of all teaching whatever (women rightly teaching children and other women, and in their own households), but specifically the public teaching of and ruling over men in the church; I suffer not a woman to teach, nor to usurp authority over the man. The witnesses note I suffer not a woman to teach, nor to usurp authority over the man. The witnesses note but to be in silence.

The witnesses note the limit — a woman not to teach or rule over men in the assembly — while her teaching of children and women in the household is rightly affirmed.

So the witnesses make the verse the restriction of public teaching and authority. The lesson the expositors draw is that the woman is not to teach or rule over men in the church, though she rightly teaches in other spheres. The witnesses note the suffer not: "I suffer not a woman to teach" — Paul does not permit a woman publicly to teach the men; the suffer not; the not permit; the publicly; the teach; the the men. The expositors observe the household teaching: Gill notes women may teach in their houses, children and good things; the household teaching; the their houses; the children; the good things; the rightly. The witnesses press the usurp authority: "nor to usurp authority over the man" — nor to exercise rule and dominion over the man; the usurp authority; the rule; the dominion; the over the man; the not. The expositors draw the lesson on the silence: "but to be in silence" — but to be in silence in the public sphere; the silence; the the public sphere; the quiet; the the assembly; the not teaching. The witnesses observe the not all teaching: this is not a forbidding of all teaching whatever; the not all teaching; the not whatever; the children; the women; the household. The expositors note the public over men: specifically the public teaching of and ruling over men in the church; the public over men; the the church; the the men; the ruling; the teaching. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I suffer not a woman to teach nor to usurp authority over the man, the but to be in silence, and the restriction of authority. The verse thus records the restriction of public teaching and authority — teaching that the woman is not to teach or rule over men in the church, though she rightly teaches in other spheres.
13:
"For Adam was first formed, then Eve." The witnesses note the argument from the order of creation. Gill notes Adam was first formed immediately by God, out of the dust of the earth, and the breath of life breathed into him; he was put into the garden, and the creatures brought to him to name, and all this while Eve was not yet formed, but afterward, and out of him. The witnesses note Paul grounding the order of the sexes in the fact that Adam was first formed, then Eve; the priority of Adam in creation as the ground of order — that Paul gives the first reason for the woman's place: the order of creation itself, "for Adam was first formed, then Eve" — Adam being made first and Eve afterward, and (as elsewhere) out of him and for him — so that the headship belongs to the man not by mere custom but by God's own ordering in the creation, a priority that grounds the submission Paul has enjoined; for Adam was first formed, then Eve. The witnesses note Adam was first formed. The witnesses note then Eve.

The witnesses note the argument from creation's order — Adam first formed, then Eve — the man's headship grounded not in custom but in God's own ordering in creation.

So the witnesses make the verse the priority of Adam in creation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the order of the sexes rests on God's creation order, Adam being formed first. The witnesses note the first formed: "for Adam was first formed" — Gill notes Adam formed first by God, out of the dust; the first formed; the Adam; the by God; the the dust; the first. The expositors observe the then Eve: "then Eve" — Eve formed afterward, and out of him; the then Eve; the afterward; the out of him; the second; the formed. The witnesses press the order of creation: the order of creation grounds the woman's place; the order of creation; the the woman's place; the grounds; the creation; the order. The expositors draw the lesson on the headship: the man's headship belongs to him by God's ordering, not custom; the headship; the the man; the God's ordering; the not custom; the creation. The witnesses observe the priority: Adam's priority in creation grounds the submission enjoined; the priority; the Adam; the grounds; the submission; the enjoined. The expositors note the by God's order: the order is by God's own ordering in the creation; the by God's order; the the creation; the God's; the ordering; the order. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Adam was first formed, the then Eve, and the priority of Adam. The verse thus records the priority of Adam in creation — teaching that the order of the sexes rests on God's creation order, Adam being formed first.
14:
"And Adam was not deceived, but the woman being deceived was in the transgression." The witnesses note the argument from the manner of the fall. Gill notes there is no need to say with interpreters that Adam was not deceived first, or not immediately, but by Eve; the apostle's positive assertion is to be taken without such limitations — Adam was not deceived by the serpent, but the woman was. The witnesses note Paul observing that Adam was not deceived, but the woman, being deceived, was in the transgression; the second reason drawn from Eve's being deceived in the fall — that Paul gives a second ground for the order he enjoins, from the history of the fall: "Adam was not deceived" (he was not the one the serpent beguiled), "but the woman being deceived was in the transgression" — Eve being the one who was deceived by the serpent and first fell into the transgression; the witnesses note this is not to make the woman the worse sinner (Adam sinned knowingly and bore the greater guilt as head), but to note that in the matter of being led astray the woman was deceived, a fact bearing on the order of teaching and rule; the woman being deceived was in the transgression. The witnesses note Adam was not deceived. The witnesses note but the woman being deceived was in the transgression.

The witnesses note the second argument — from the fall: Adam was not deceived, but the woman, being deceived, fell into transgression; bearing on the order of teaching and rule.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument from Eve's deception. The lesson the expositors draw is that the woman was the one deceived in the fall, a fact Paul applies to the order of teaching and rule. The witnesses note the not deceived: "Adam was not deceived" — Gill notes Adam was not deceived by the serpent; the not deceived; the Adam; the not the serpent; the not beguiled; the assertion. The expositors observe the the woman deceived: "but the woman being deceived" — the woman was deceived by the serpent; the the woman deceived; the the serpent; the beguiled; the led astray; the Eve. The witnesses press the in the transgression: "was in the transgression" — and first fell into the transgression; the in the transgression; the first fell; the the transgression; the the fall; the sin. The expositors draw the lesson on the not the worse sinner: this does not make the woman the worse sinner (Adam sinned knowingly, bearing the greater guilt); the not the worse sinner; the Adam knowingly; the greater guilt; the as head; the not worse. The witnesses observe the led astray: in being led astray, the woman was the one deceived; the led astray; the the woman; the deceived; the the matter; the beguiled. The expositors note the bears on order: this fact bears on the order of teaching and rule; the bears on order; the the order; the teaching; the rule; the applied. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Adam was not deceived, the but the woman being deceived was in the transgression, and the argument from the fall. The verse thus records the argument from Eve's deception — teaching that the woman was the one deceived in the fall, a fact Paul applies to the order of teaching and rule.
15:
"Notwithstanding she shall be saved in childbearing, if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety." The witnesses note the much-debated promise of being saved in childbearing. Gill notes this is not meant of Eve, though she is doubtless saved, but rather of any woman, particularly such as profess godliness, who shall be saved. Barnes notes the promise is designed to alleviate the apparent severity of the preceding remarks. The witnesses note Paul declaring that she shall be saved in childbearing, if they continue in faith, charity, and holiness with sobriety; the comfort to women amid the curse, the much-debated "saved in childbearing" — that Paul, having spoken of the woman's part in the transgression, adds a word of comfort and hope; the witnesses note the debate on its meaning — whether it means the woman shall be brought safely through the perils of childbirth (a temporal preservation), or shall be saved (eternally) notwithstanding the sorrow of childbearing laid on her in the curse, or "saved through THE childbearing" (the bringing forth of the Messiah, the seed of the woman) — and Paul adds the condition marking the truly saved, "if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety," so that the woman's hope, like every believer's, is bound up with persevering faith and a holy life; she shall be saved in childbearing. The witnesses note she shall be saved in childbearing. The witnesses note if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety.

The witnesses note the comforting yet much-debated "saved in childbearing" — read as safe passage through childbirth, salvation despite the curse, or through THE Childbearing of the Messiah.

So the witnesses make the verse the comfort to women amid the curse. The lesson the expositors draw is that, however "saved in childbearing" be read, the woman's hope is bound up with persevering faith and a holy life. The witnesses note the comfort: "she shall be saved in childbearing" — Barnes notes this alleviates the preceding severity; the comfort; the alleviates; the the severity; the hope; the word. The expositors present the debate: the witnesses note the debate — whether she is brought safely through childbirth (temporal preservation), or saved eternally notwithstanding the sorrow of childbearing in the curse, or saved through THE childbearing (the birth of the Messiah, the woman's seed). The witnesses press the not Eve: Gill notes it is not meant of Eve only, but of any godly woman; the not Eve; the any woman; the godly; the saved; the professing. The expositors draw the lesson on the condition: "if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety" — the condition marking the truly saved; the the condition; the continue; the faith; the charity; the holiness. The witnesses observe the persevering faith: the woman's hope is bound up with persevering faith; the persevering faith; the her hope; the bound up; the faith; the continue. The expositors note the holy life: and a holy life, with sobriety; the holy life; the holiness; the sobriety; the bound; the persevere. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the she shall be saved in childbearing, the if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with sobriety, and the comfort amid the curse. The verse thus records the comfort to women amid the curse — teaching that, however read, the woman's hope is bound up with persevering faith and a holy life.
